description: Scientists modeled a flow where sound gets trapped and found that entanglement entropy grows with volume, not area. The reason: pairs of sound particles (phonons), born at the horizon, remain connected throughout the interior. This helps us understand how information might be preserved inside real b
abstract: Researchers studied an acoustic black hole (a flow where sound gets trapped) and discovered that quantum entanglement inside it grows with volume, not surface area. Imagine cobwebs packing a whole ball, not just coating its shell. Why? It's all about paired sound waves born at the horizon.

title: The Swirling Dance of Neutron Stars
oneliner: The spin of neutron stars in their deadly dance determines whether a narrow beam or a cloud of debris appears.
description: New simulations show how neutron star spin changes the merger outcome. Aligned spins birth a narrow jet, opposite spins — chaos. Ghostly neutrinos turn the ejecta into proton-rich matter, where nickel-56 ignites. Its glow reveals a long-lived remnant.
abstract: Imagine two spinning tops colliding: if they were spinning in the same direction, a focused jet rich in nickel-56 is born, whose later decay gives a bright flash. If in opposite directions — the impact is destructive, and a powerful magnetic wind gets stuck in the debris. That’s how the spin of neutron stars decides what kind of cosmic 'fireworks' we see.

title: How a Black Hole Amplifies Gravitational Waves
oneliner: Physicists have discovered that a black hole with a vibrating shell acts as a natural resonator, multiplying weak signals.
description: A cloud of matter can orbit a black hole, and the space between them works like a natural resonator. Weak external disturbances shake a hidden field in this gap, and its energy grows like an avalanche over time. Once amplified, the field emits a secondary gravitational wave — a delayed but powerful
abstract: Picture a swing that picks up energy from perfectly timed pushes. Something similar happens around a black hole with gravitational waves: a vibrating shell acts as the push, and the spacetime around the hole is the swing. Even weak waves get boosted and create a delayed burst. It makes you wonder—what other cosmic signals get amplified by this kind of resonance?

title: Tunnels from Entropy: A New Key to Wormholes
oneliner: Scientists have shown that modified forms of entropy naturally spawn the exotic matter needed for traversable wormholes.
description: Can modified entropy laws replace exotic matter for wormholes? Testing five non-standard models, physicists found each naturally generates matter with negative energy density—exactly what keeps spacetime tunnels open. This links entropic gravity theory to the prospect of interstellar travel.
abstract: Scientists checked whether altered thermal behavior rules of black holes can create traversable wormholes – tunnels in space. Just as different dough recipes yield different pastries, different thermal models of black holes give rise to wormholes with different properties. Could the cosmos be full of such hidden passages?

title: The Universe Doesn't Remember Its Youth: The Echo of Quantum Gravity Can't Be Caught
oneliner: Scientists tested whether events right after the Big Bang could affect the current expansion of the cosmos. The answer: no.
description: Cosmologists searched for the imprint of the tumultuous birth of the universe in galaxy distance data and the afterglow of the Big Bang. But all measurements fit perfectly into the standard model, with no 'memory'. If a trace exists, it's too faint for current instruments.
abstract: Imagine the universe, like a memory foam mattress, could retain imprints of quantum processes from its birth. Scientists tested this using data on cosmic expansion and found: there's no 'memory,' everything follows the standard model. Perhaps quantum traces vanish faster than we thought?

title: Gravitational Waves: Listening to Space without Templates
oneliner: The ticking of distant pulsars gives away any gravitational waves — even unexpected ones, because the new method doesn't require guessing their shape.
description: Pulsars are cosmic lighthouses beaming radio pulses with the precision of atomic clocks. A passing gravitational wave disrupts this rhythm. Instead of searching for familiar patterns, the new technique listens to all the noise and picks out any signal, decomposing it into frequencies and applying a
abstract: Scientists compare pulsars to cosmic lighthouses, whose pulses arrive on a strict schedule. Gravitational waves slightly disrupt this rhythm. The new method searches for such disruptions without ready-made templates, listening closely to any unusual 'noise' — like recognizing a familiar melody amid interference. This will help detect even signals no one expects. What else is the Universe whispering to us?

title: Rotating Wormhole Without Exotic Matter
oneliner: Rotation itself holds the wormhole open — no antigravity needed.
description: Scientists considered a theory of gravity where matter curves space more strongly. In this model, rotation prevents the wormhole from collapsing, eliminating the need for exotic matter. Since almost all cosmic bodies rotate, such tunnels could form naturally. They would be betrayed by a double shado
abstract: Imagine space as a sheet of paper. A wormhole is a tunnel between two distant points, but normally exotic matter is needed to keep it stable. In this work, they showed that in a modified theory of gravity, a rotating wormhole can exist with ordinary matter, without any weirdness. Could such cosmic tunnels really be out there, waiting to be discovered?

title: The Key to Quantum Gravity Lies in a Single Atom
oneliner: The dance of a single atom under gravity can confirm the quantum nature of gravity.
description: Physicists have found a workaround: instead of complex experiments to entangle massive objects, you can track the motion of a single atom. If its quantum dance obeys the Schrödinger equation, two atoms automatically become entangled through gravity. This brings us closer to answering the question ab
abstract: Scientists have come up with a clever way to test whether gravity is quantum. If we measure how a single particle, existing in multiple places at once, is attracted to a heavy object, and it matches the calculations, that indirectly proves: gravity can entangle objects, like two synchronized dancers. Perhaps the quantum world of gravity is closer than it seems?

title: Cosmic Hide-and-Seek: Webb Spots a Planet Near a Neighboring Star
oneliner: The James Webb Space Telescope briefly caught a signal from object S1 — a possible planet around Alpha Centauri A, which then immediately vanished.
description: At Alpha Centauri A, the nearest Sun-like star, Webb briefly picked up a dot called S1 — a planet candidate the size of Jupiter but with a temperature around –20°C. The object hid behind the star — its orbit lasts just 2–3 years, and there’s remarkably little dust around.
abstract: Astronomers gazed at the nearest solar neighbor—Alpha Centauri A—using the James Webb Space Telescope. They were looking for planets and dust but found a mysterious object: it appeared in one observation and vanished in the next. Could it be a planet hiding behind the star, like a coin that’s now visible, now not?

title: Photonic Quantum Computer Sets a Speed Record
oneliner: The Jiuzhang 4.0 quantum processor handles in microseconds a task that would take the most powerful supercomputer 10^42 years.
description: The new photonic computer Jiuzhang 4.0 pushes photons through a network of microchannels and uses photometry—precise counting of light flashes—to record the final pattern. This pattern is beyond any ordinary computer: it would require sifting through more possibilities than atoms in the universe. Th
abstract: Chinese scientists have built a new-generation quantum computer that solves a tricky problem using light. If the world's most powerful supercomputer tried to calculate it, it would need billions of times longer than the universe has existed. Jiuzhang 4.0 does it in a split second. It's like finding a path through a maze where all the walls vanish on command. What else will this technology reveal to us?

title: Dark Energy: How a New Survey Changes the Picture
oneliner: A map of millions of galaxies hints: dark energy may be changing its strength. This threatens the Universe with a different end.
description: The DESI survey examined the motion of galaxies and supernovae, testing the constancy of dark energy. Faint signals point to its evolution, and the Mirage model gave the best but not definitive result. If the energy is indeed growing, the Universe could face a tearing apart of matter.
abstract: Dark energy is a mysterious force that makes the Universe expand faster and faster. Scientists checked whether it has changed over time using the latest galaxy map (DESI) and supernova data. Hints of change exist, but they are too faint and uncertain, like a whisper in a noisy room. Maybe dark energy does evolve, but is the cosmos playing hide-and-seek with us?

title: Sunlight replaces lasers: quantum imaging without complex optics
oneliner: Physicists have learned to split sunlight into pairs of quantum twins and obtained an image for the first time using only solar illumination instead of lasers.
description: Sunlight is passed through a crystal that splits each photon into two entangled halves. Their invisible link allows imaging where ordinary optics are blind: in fog, under murky water, in near-total darkness. Previously, only lasers could do this—bulky and expensive. Now, daylight can be the source,
abstract: Scientists have used sunlight for quantum imaging—a method that usually requires lasers. Imagine an ordinary flashlight suddenly revealing hidden details of objects. This paves the way for using scattered light in quantum technologies and may simplify space communications. Could we really see the invisible without complicated equipment?

title: Microwave Teleportation Withstands the Heat
oneliner: Quantum internet without total deep-freeze: teleportation of microwave signals achieved for the first time in a 'hot' channel at 4 K.
description: Connecting superconducting quantum computers is hindered by lethal heat. The solution is teleportation via entangled signals. In an experiment, the channel was heated to 4 K, yet fidelity remained above the classical limit. Now, distributed quantum networks can be built without cooling everything do
abstract: Scientists teleported faint microwave signals from one ultra-cold fridge to another through a hot, noisy channel. Imagine passing a whisper through a rumbling hose, yet quantum "entanglement" let them recover the voice with fidelity beyond classical limits. At 4 Kelvin (a frosty –269°C), they hit 60% accuracy—a quantum win. Could we link quantum computers this way in the future?

title: Interstellar Visitor with Carbonated Breath
oneliner: An object from another star system releases carbon dioxide like an open soda bottle, hiding its core in a cloud of dust.
description: The SPHEREx telescope split the light of the interstellar wanderer 3I ATLAS into colors. It revealed that it's actively emitting carbon dioxide, while water ice absorbs some of the rays like tinted glass. All visible glow is a dusty shell, with the solid core inside being hundreds of times smaller.
abstract: Scientists examined the interstellar object 3I ATLAS and saw that its bright cloud of dust and gas hides a tiny nucleus — like a dense fog concealing a lantern. It seems such visitors from other star systems are much smaller than they appear. I wonder how many more of these wanderers we’ll be able to spot?

title: Black Hole Wind Clears the Galactic Center
oneliner: For the first time, scientists have seen the supermassive black hole at the Milky Way’s heart unleashing a hot wind.
description: Astronomers found the 'breath' of the black hole at the center of the Milky Way. A huge cone of blown-out cold hydrogen, over three light-years long, proved that black holes not only devour matter but also clear space with powerful winds.
abstract: At the center of our Galaxy lies a supermassive black hole. Scientists have discovered that it 'blows out' a huge cone in the surrounding gas cloud, like a hairdryer pushing aside fluff. This discovery helps us understand how black holes affect their galaxies. What else might the heart of the Milky Way be hiding?

title: Hunting for Light from Black Hole Crumbs
oneliner: Scientists tried to spot instantaneous flashes from tiny black holes born in giant collisions, but without success so far.
description: Black hole mergers can create tiny mini black holes that quickly evaporate, flashing with gamma-ray light. A search for such signals in the Fermi telescope archive came up empty, but it allowed to rule out certain masses for these crumbs.
abstract: Scientists looked for Hawking radiation from minuscule black holes that might be born when big ones crash together. Using data from the Fermi telescope, they watched the spot where black holes merged. They found nothing, but they were able to set limits on how tiny these holes can be. Picture an asteroid melting like an ice cube and giving off invisible light—so far, this light stays out of sight. Will we ever catch a glimpse of this cosmic vapor?

title: Two Probes Will Dive into the Tail of an Interstellar Comet
oneliner: In late October 2025, the Europa Clipper and Hera spacecraft will find themselves inside the tail of Comet 3I/ATLAS.
description: A comet from another star system will pass by two Earth spacecraft. In late October, they will spend several days in its tail—this is the first chance to directly study extrasolar material.
abstract: In late 2025, a comet from another star system will fly near two scientific probes. The probes might pass through its tail — like a boat cutting through a ship’s wake, but made of charged particles. This gives a unique chance to directly ‘touch’ material that came from far away. What will we learn about this cosmic wanderer?

title: Earth's Shadow Hunts for Alien Probes
oneliner: Scientists have proposed a new way to search for extraterrestrial artifacts, using Earth's shadow as a filter.
description: Astronomers tested the idea of using Earth's shadow to detect alien probes. An automated telescope captured brief light flashes near the shadow's edge — objects not listed in any catalog. This could change the approach to the search for extraterrestrial intelligence.
abstract: Scientists have proposed hunting for alien objects near Earth using our planet's shadow. The shadow acts as a filter: inside it, the sky is darker, and dim artificial objects like probes become more visible. Early observations with the ZTF telescope have already uncovered unknown objects. What if alien emissaries are already here, but we just weren't looking in the right place?

title: Superfluid Helium Feels How the Earth Spins
oneliner: A lab experiment will capture how Earth twists space-time.
description: Earth drags the fabric of space-time along with it — an effect predicted by Einstein. Scientists propose catching it with superfluid helium and a highly precise detector. The device will notice rotation slower than one revolution in the entire history of Earth.
abstract: An ultra-precise gyroscope based on quantum fluid helium can detect how the rotating Earth twists spacetime around it — an effect predicted by Einstein. It works like a spinning top, sensing the tiniest shudder from the planet "dragging" space. In just one second, it can notice a rotation a billion times slower than an hour hand. What would you feel if you could sense the rotation of the universe itself?

title: Tantalum Nucleus Reveals Electric Asymmetry
oneliner: Tantalum’s nucleus: a tiny skew that saved the Universe.
description: An electric dipole moment has been spotted in the tantalum nucleus — like a spinning top’s center of mass suddenly shifting. This imbalance, measured with record precision, sheds light on the mystery of missing antimatter and aids the hunt for dark matter.
abstract: Imagine the atomic nucleus not as a perfect sphere, but with a tiny electrical 'imbalance'. Scientists have measured such an imbalance—the electric dipole moment—for the nucleus of tantalum-181, using a superconducting current. This discovery could explain why there is more matter than antimatter in the Universe.

title: Dark Energy Is Not Constant: A New Look at the Universe's Acceleration
oneliner: Data from the DESI survey suggests that the mysterious force speeding up the cosmos may not be eternal or constant.
description: Analysis of galaxy maps, quasar light, and supernovae shows that the simple model of constant dark energy is losing to a version with a smoothly changing force. This needs verification, but it's intriguing.
abstract: Scientists used data from a new telescope to study dark energy—the mysterious force accelerating the expansion of the Universe. They found hints that dark energy might change over time, like a wind that strengthens and weakens, rather than staying constant. Is the fate of the Universe written in wavering shadows, or is it just a trick of the light?

title: Tiny Seesaw Cooled Almost to Absolute Zero
oneliner: Physicists cooled a microscopic seesaw to 0.006 degrees above absolute zero—so cold that its swinging almost stopped, but not quite.
description: Using nuclear demagnetization—a method that extracts energy by removing a magnetic field—a 1.5-nanogram plate was passively cooled to 6.1 millikelvin. The residual jittering neatly obeyed the laws of thermodynamics. The record paves the way for gravitational wave detectors and testing quantum mechan
abstract: A tiny vibrating plate, weighing about as much as a couple of cells, was cooled nearly to absolute zero (down to just 0.006 degrees above) to calm its thermal jitters. Now it can pick up the faintest forces. What if, with its help, we could hear the motion of a single atom?

title: The Hunt for Moons of Rogue Planets
oneliner: For the first time, the James Webb Space Telescope tested a method to find moons around lonely planets drifting without stars.
description: Astronomers analyzed data on the orphan planet WISE 0855 — a dim, cold ball not far away. For 11 hours, Webb watched its light, trying to spot the shadow of a moon. No direct discovery happened, but the method proved its worth. If many rogue planets have Titan-sized moons, we will soon find them.
abstract: Scientists used the JWST to hunt for moons around the solitary planet WISE 0855—like a wandering Earth without a star. Like a flashlight, the telescope caught shadows of passing moons, but found none. However, they calculated: if you check several such planets, the chance to spot a moon is high. How many lonely planets hide moons in the dark?

title: Quantum Chip Outperforms Supercomputers: Two Hours vs. Years
oneliner: An experiment on a quantum processor showed it solves a specialized task in hours, while classical computers would take years.
description: Scientists created quantum circuits that produce a specific result with high probability. The H2 quantum processor tackled the most complex one in two hours. The best classical simulation methods on supercomputers would take several years. This is a practical leap in quantum supremacy and opens new
abstract: Scientists ran a special program on a quantum computer that gives the right result in just a couple of hours—like instantly finding the one key that fits from a pile. Regular supercomputers would take years to do the same. This is another step toward quantum supremacy. What if this technology one day protects our data?

title: Quantum Predator-Prey Games
oneliner: Physicists had atoms act out the predator-prey drama on a quantum lattice.
description: The Lotka-Volterra model, familiar from the population fluctuations of foxes and hares, now works in the quantum world. Scientists lined up giant atoms and made them simulate population battles. Quantum effects don't disrupt but sustain these cycles — a path to the ultrafast simulators of the future
abstract: Scientists have created a quantum analog of the predator-prey model (like foxes and rabbits) on an array of Rydberg atoms. On microsecond timescales, atomic excitations play the roles of predators and prey, exhibiting stable cycles. This opens the door to simulating ecosystems in quantum labs. Could other ecological rhythms emerge in atomic "jungles"?

title: How to Find Life: Look for Molecular Diversity
oneliner: A new method for detecting life assesses not specific substances, but the chemical diversity of a sample.
description: To find life beyond Earth, you don't need to guess what it's made of. A new method evaluates not specific molecules, but the overall chemical diversity of a sample. Living organisms typically produce a rich cocktail of amino acids (the building blocks of proteins) and fatty acids, while non-living n
abstract: Imagine: if a flowerpot contains many different seeds, it's likely something grew there. Scientists have applied a similar approach to searching for life on other planets—they measure not the presence of specific molecules, but the overall diversity of "building blocks" in samples. It turns out that living systems are always richer in composition than non-living ones, even after mimicking harsh space conditions. So, can we find life where we didn't expect it?

title: Warm Moons of Rogue Planets
oneliner: New research shows that moons of ejected planets can sustain liquid oceans thanks to ongoing gravitational heating.
description: Simulations show that after a planet is ejected by a supernova explosion, all its moons are left on slightly elongated orbits. This triggers constant internal heating — as if the planet endlessly kneads its moons. In 12–15% of cases, the heat is enough for subsurface oceans, like those on Europa and
abstract: Orphan planets, ejected by a stellar explosion, can hold onto their moons. These moons undergo constant squeezing and stretching (like kneading clay), which heats them from within. This heat can sustain liquid oceans under an icy crust — and thus, conditions for life. Could life have emerged there?

title: The Moon — a Giant Gravitational Bell
oneliner: Like a bell, the Moon rings from gravitational waves, and scientists have found the spot where this ringing is heard best.
description: Computer modeling has shown that the Moon's uneven terrain and crust thickness turn its surface into an amplifier of spacetime ripples. In the region of the South Pole–Aitken basin, the signal is amplified dozens of times, paving the way for future lunar observatories capable of detecting the merger
abstract: The Moon acts like a musical instrument for gravitational waves—some parts of it 'sound' especially loud. Scientists have discovered that in areas with the thickest crust, around the South Pole, the amplification can be tens of times. Imagine a quiet whisper turning into an audible voice—isn't that a wonder?

title: How a Whirlpool in a Cup Becomes a Black Hole Bomb
oneliner: An ordinary vortex in a glass of water turned out to be a miniature copy of a black hole bomb.
description: New research shows that if you swirl water vigorously in a shallow dish, a vortex with an empty center forms. Waves on its surface, reflecting off the edges, accumulate energy — exactly like around a rotating black hole. This vortex became the first desktop model for a dangerous cosmic phenomenon —
abstract: A vortex in a fluid can mimic a black hole. If you put it in a closed vessel, reflected waves can rock the system to instability — like a 'black hole bomb'. New research shows that vortices with fluid expelled from the center are best suited for such imitation. Can an ordinary whirlpool become a window into gravitational physics?

title: How Time Can Run Backwards
oneliner: In the quantum world, the arrow of time behaves like a coin: it can land on heads and tails at the same time.
description: Scientists have figured out how to make quantum clocks tick in both directions at once. To do this, a magnetic particle is placed in a trap made of opposing fields — and it feels the flow of time backwards just as real as forwards. Such an experiment would test temporal entanglement and show that an
abstract: Time usually only flows forward, but the quantum world allows reverse motion too – like a film reel that can be run backward. Scientists have proposed 'quantum clocks' made of a spinning particle in a magnetic field, which can tick forward and backward at the same time. Such a 'superposition of times' allows past and future to interfere. What if the arrow of time itself is a quantum object?

title: A Tabletop Light Maze Captures Gravity
oneliner: A 50-kilometer fiber-optic labyrinth on a table has enabled the first direct measurement of how gravity shifts the wave of a single photon.
description: For the first time in a lab, researchers directly measured how gravity alters the path of single photons. They built a tabletop labyrinth from 50 km of fiber. The device compared the paths of two halves of a photon wave and detected a delay of millionths of a radian—a result of spacetime curvature.
abstract: A 50-kilometer fiber-optic interferometer built on a table catches tiny photon shifts caused by gravity—like spotting a hair bending across a football field. It's a chance to test quantum effects in curved spacetime. Could unexpected phenomena pop up?

title: Warp Drive Without Turbulence: A Cheaper Path to the Stars?
oneliner: A new warp drive model requires thousands of times less negative energy than previous ones.
description: A new warp drive model uses a smooth curvature of spacetime without turbulence and requires tens of times less negative energy than previous versions: now a tiny energy imbalance is enough.
abstract: Scientists have come up with a new warp drive design without vortices, like a smoothly flowing stream. This drive requires much less exotic matter — almost as if a handful of "fuel" could carry us to the stars. Could this bring interstellar travel within reach?

title: Gravity Can Push Away — A Quantum Experiment
oneliner: Scientists have proposed an experiment where gravity exhibits repulsion due to the quantum nature of the field.
description: Ordinary gravity only attracts. But if the source of gravity is in two places at once, you can make it push a test particle. This is impossible in classical physics and serves as proof of gravity's quantum nature.
abstract: Gravity always attracts, but scientists have devised an experiment where quantum superposition (an object in two places at once) leads to gravitational repulsion. Such a trick is impossible without quantum interference and would prove gravity's quantum nature. What else is hiding in the familiar world?

title: Sunlight Creates Quantum Entanglement
oneliner: Ordinary sunlight entangles particles as well as lab lasers.
description: Physicists have discovered that ordinary sunlight is enough for quantum entanglement. By passing it through a crystal, they obtained pairs of 'twin particles' that instantly sense each other. Measurements confirmed that the link is on par with laser systems. This paves the way for cheap quantum tech
abstract: Turns out, you don't need lasers to create quantum entanglement: ordinary sunlight gives birth to photon pairs with super-strong correlations. They violate the strict classical limit (Bell's inequality), confirming their quantum nature. It's like switching from spotlights to natural lighting: quantum technologies become more energy-efficient. Could the quantum internet one day run on sunlight?

title: Warp Drives Ranked: The Gentlest Way to Bend Spacetime
oneliner: A new universal test reveals which warp designs disturb spacetime the least, making faster-than-light travel slightly less impossible.
description: Scientists built a toolkit that checks warp drives for any observer, even at warp speeds. It ranks them by how much they violate nature's energy laws. The Rodal design is the calmest, Alcubierre's the wildest. This steers research toward more realistic warp concepts.
abstract: Imagine space is a rubber sheet and a warp drive stretches it. It turns out some designs barely tear the 'rubber' of reality, while others do so constantly. Scientists found a universal way to test this, and the gentlest option — the Rodal-type drive — is 70 times 'softer' than the popular Alcubierre. Will we ever travel faster than light without breaking physics?

title: How a Swallowed Planet Spun Up a Star
oneliner: A gas giant that fell into a star made it spin with youthful speed.
description: The star GJ 504, similar to the Sun, spins too fast for its age. After devouring a Jupiter-sized planet, it gained extra angular momentum and regained its magnetic youth. A similar case with HD 75332 shows that stellar cannibalism is not uncommon.
abstract: Scientists studied the star GJ 504, which looks too young for its age: it spins fast and has a strong magnetic field. It turns out it might have swallowed a nearby giant planet, giving it a jolt of energy—like a morning coffee. Could our Sun ever meet a similar fate?

title: Dyson Spheres Around White and Red Dwarfs: Calculating the Search
oneliner: Scientists have calculated how giant 'blankets' around stars — Dyson spheres — would glow, to help telescopes find traces of aliens.
description: A Dyson sphere is a cosmic blanket that captures all of a star's energy. Scientists have figured out how such a blanket glows around white and red dwarfs: the bigger it is, the cooler it gets. Surprisingly, the hottest blanket comes from the dimmest star. These clues will aid telescopes in the hunt
abstract: Picture a sphere that catches all the light from a star. Scientists have figured out that if you build one around a dim white or red dwarf, it would be invisible to the naked eye, but would give itself away with a faint heat glow in the infrared. Will we ever spot something like that through a telescope?

title: Why a Tent on Mars Beats a Full-Blown Planetary Makeover
oneliner: New research shows: turning Mars into an Earth clone is like gut-renovating an abandoned house from scratch. But living under a dome-tent could happen within the foreseeable future.
description: Making Mars warm and with breathable air would require energy comparable to all of Earth's factories working for hundreds of years. The mirrors needed for heating would cover an area the size of Africa. Instead of a global overhaul, scientists propose building massive dome-tents over cozy valleys —
abstract: Scientists have calculated whether Mars could be made warm and breathable. It turned out to be like trying to fill an ocean with a garden hose: staggering reserves of gases and energy are needed. Instead, it's more realistic to create enclosed oases. Why chase an entire planet when you can start small?

title: Quantum Telepathy: Agreement Without Communication or Delay
oneliner: Entangled particles act like telepaths: their states match instantly across any distance.
description: Entangled particles work like a pair of dice that always land on the same face. This allows coordinating actions without communication: traders synchronize deals, rescuers coordinate routes. Nature manages without signals, and mathematics guarantees a win over classical schemes. Devices for this alr
abstract: Quantum telepathy allows parties to instantly coordinate actions without communication — like two musicians playing in unison without hearing each other. This could speed up stock trading or help rescuers find a lost hiker. Imagine: drones searching for a person and sharing data without delays — how much faster would help arrive?

title: Interstellar Comet: Water 14 Times Heavier Than Earth's
oneliner: The James Webb Telescope found a record amount of deuterium—heavy hydrogen—in the interstellar object 3I/ATLAS.
description: Spectroscopic analysis of comet 3I/ATLAS by Webb revealed an anomalously high deuterium content. This chemical marker suggests the comet was born in the bitter cold of a distant star system.
abstract: Imagine a package from another star system that tells you how planets were born there. Scientists saw for the first time in the visiting comet 3I/ATLAS an unusually large amount of 'heavy' methane, which is like a pass to the cold outskirts of an alien world. It makes you wonder: if we built a ship to such a system, what would we find there?

title: How Sound Helped Find a Space Rock Over Alaska
oneliner: A daytime meteor over Alaska was detected not by light, but by sound and ground tremors.
description: A meteor missed by satellite cameras was reconstructed using sound and vibrations. A low-cost alternative for polar latitudes, where the never-setting sun blinds optics.
abstract: On April 24, 2025, a brilliant fireball flew over Alaska. Dozens of ground sensors picked up the earth and air vibrations, precisely tracing the trajectory and power of the event. It's like a forest that 'detects' a passing object by the rustle of leaves. In the Arctic, where satellites often can't see, these networks prove indispensable. The Earth can 'hear' space better than the sky!

title: All Atoms Fall Alike: A Cosmic Verdict
oneliner: A space test with atoms confirmed: gravity pulls everything equally, to within one ten-millionth.
description: The idea that all objects fall equally underpins Einstein's theory. On the Chinese space station, physicists compared the fall of two types of rubidium atoms. The result matched the prediction with an error of a few ten-millionths.
abstract: Einstein believed that gravity treats everything equally. Scientists put this to the test in space by dropping clouds of two types of rubidium atoms and measuring their fall using quantum interference. It turned out that even in Earth's orbit, the difference in their motion is no more than billionths. Could we detect even the slightest deviation from perfect equality?

title: Quantum Abacus: Cracking Codes with 10,000 Atoms
oneliner: It turns out that a quantum computer made of ten thousand atoms, not millions, is enough to crack modern encryption.
description: Previous estimates demanded millions of qubits for quantum decryption. New research shows that just 10,000 movable atoms, like abacus beads, will do. With laser tweezers and error-correcting codes, RSA-2048 could be broken in days.
abstract: Quantum computers using atoms in laser traps could one day break modern encryption. Previously, this required millions of qubits, but now scientists say: 10,000 are enough, and with 26,000, cracking a key would take days. It's like a complex safe opening at a mere touch. Are our data really at risk?

title: Quantum Computers Will Challenge Gravity
oneliner: Testing quantum gravity needs just 500 qubits in a lab, or 1,600 on a cosmic scale.
description: Relativity and quantum mechanics diverge at the micro level. A new approach: if a quantum computer surpasses the classical speed limit of computation, it becomes a test of quantum gravity. A lab needs 500 logical qubits, the cosmos—1,600. Commercial roadmaps promise to reach that milestone soon.
abstract: Quantum computers can test whether gravity and the quantum world are compatible. Just as an ultra-high-speed camera catches a bullet, a quantum computer with 500–1600 logical qubits (basic cells) could 'see' the limit where spacetime becomes grainy. Commercial plans are already close to this — soon the mystery will be solved.

title: Quantum Rainbow: How a Tiny Computer Sorts Mountains of Data
oneliner: A quantum algorithm using 60 qubits tackles data analysis that would demand planetary-scale resources with ordinary computers.
description: Researchers have proved that even a tiny quantum processor can outperform massive classical computers in sorting and analyzing big data. The 'quantum sketch' algorithm was successfully used to decode genes in individual cells and to gauge the sentiment of movie reviews. All of this was accomplished
abstract: Imagine a tiny sieve that can instantly separate wheat from chaff in an ocean of data — a task that would take an ordinary computer immense power and time. Scientists have shown that a quantum computer with a handful of qubits does the same thing, processing data on the fly. They confirmed the idea with real examples: DNA sequencing and sentiment analysis of reviews. So could quantum mechanics become the new standard of efficiency?

title: Planetary Orbits: Not Random, But Music
oneliner: Astronomers have noticed: exoplanets sit at strictly defined distances from their star, like notes on a guitar string.
description: Analysis of exoplanet surveys showed that their orbits are grouped, not scattered randomly. It's like piano keys: there are preferred positions and gaps in between. The cause is standing waves in the gas-dust disks that dictate the birthplaces of worlds. The discovery changes our view of planetary s
abstract: A hundred years ago, physics changed when it was discovered that electrons in an atom don't move randomly but occupy fixed, quantum levels. Now there are hints that planet orbits around distant stars might also be quantized, like steps on a staircase. The cause is giant standing waves in the gas and dust disks where planets are born. Could it be that even in space, everything is aligned with invisible rulers?

title: Caught: The Light That Lit Up the Young Universe
oneliner: For the first time, astronomers have directly observed the radiation that cleared the cosmic fog after the Big Bang.
description: Telescopes have for the first time captured light that knocks electrons out of atoms, coming from distant galaxies about a billion years old. The intergalactic medium allowed nearly 80% of ultraviolet light to pass through—far more transparent than expected. This changes our understanding of how the
abstract: Scientists have captured light from the early Universe that rips electrons off hydrogen atoms. This light slipped through space more easily than expected—like fog that was thinner than we thought. That means very hot stars were already blazing back then. What other secrets hide at the dawn of time?

title: Thorium Nuclear Clock Ticks with a Whisper of Light
oneliner: Scientists excited a thorium nucleus with an ultra-weak continuous laser, bringing us a step closer to the most stable clock ever.
description: The article describes a new way to excite the nucleus of thorium-229 using a very weak, steady laser beam. Instead of waiting for the nucleus to glow, the team detected the laser light absorbed by the crystal, making the signal faster and clearer. This opens the door to a solid-state nuclear clock t
abstract: Physicists have for the first time excited a nuclear transition in thorium-229 using a continuous laser beam with less than a nanowatt of power. It's like setting a supremely precise pendulum in motion, but inside an atomic nucleus. This method speeds up signal readout, bringing us closer to creating ultra-stable nuclear clocks. Imagine a clock that wouldn't lose a second over the entire age of the universe — perhaps it could open doors to new physics.

title: Nuclear Clock: The Ticking Nucleus in the Search for Dark Matter
oneliner: The first nuclear clock is ticking: instead of electrons, the thorium nucleus does the ticking. This opens the door to hunting dark matter.
description: Physicists have built a clock where the pendulum is an atomic nucleus. Its ticking is almost immune to disturbances. It's accurate to 10⁻¹⁵ seconds per day, setting new limits on dark matter particles.
abstract: Scientists have built the first working nuclear clock powered by thorium-229. Unlike ordinary atomic clocks, it harnesses vibrations not of electrons but of the nucleus itself—like the atom’s heartbeat, far more resistant to interference. This paves the way for ultra-precise timekeeping and the hunt for dark matter. Picture this: the very fabric of spacetime could shift the rhythm of such a clock.

title: Mysterious Trace on Titan's Surface
oneliner: The James Webb Telescope has discovered an unexplained chemical trace on Titan and Pluto.
description: Titan's dense haze long concealed its surface. But in infrared light, the JWST telescope discerned a narrow dark band — a chemical fingerprint of an unknown substance. A similar, but broader, one was found on Pluto. This discovery brings us closer to unraveling the composition of distant icy worlds.
abstract: Imagine looking at a painting through a fogged-up window — that's how Titan's thick atmosphere obscures its surface. Using the James Webb Space Telescope, astronomers peered through a special 'transparency window' and discovered a mysterious dip in infrared light at a wavelength of 5.113 µm, likely coming from the surface itself. What substance left this mark? Not yet clear, but it's like finding an alien chemist's fingerprint on the foggy glass.

title: Visitors from the Abyss: How Passing Planets Shaped Earth's Fate
oneliner: A planet flying past Earth could trigger floods, eruptions, and the death of the dinosaurs.
description: Hundreds of rogue planets lurk on the outskirts of the Solar System. Their close encounters with Earth—even without a collision—cause waves, eruptions, and climate shifts. This explains past mass extinctions.
abstract: On the outskirts of the solar system, a multitude of planet-sized objects could be hiding. Sometimes they swing close to Earth, stirring up gigantic waves and eruptions—as if a celestial body rushed past. Perhaps it’s these encounters that explain the mass extinctions of the past. Picture an ocean heaved upward by a passing 'cosmic mountain'?

title: The Earth may survive when the Sun becomes a giant
oneliner: New calculations: Earth likely won't burn up when the Sun turns into a red giant — it will move away to a safe distance.
description: The main enemy here is not heat, but gravity. If the Sun sheds mass fast enough, Earth will retreat to a more distant orbit. Judging by observations of similar stars, we have a pretty good chance.
abstract: As the Sun ages, Earth's fate hinges on two invisible forces: how much the Sun inflates and how tightly it grips the planets with its gravity. New calculations show Earth might live to tell the tale—like a boat weathering a storm—if the 'wind' of the solar wind stays mild. But just how solid is this hope?

title: Warp Drive: Turning Without Exotic Matter
oneliner: You don't need exotic matter to steer a warp drive—just powerful radiation and a readiness to shed mass.
description: Physicists have found a way to steer a warp drive without resorting to fantastical negative-energy matter. The ship ejects light or gravitational waves, like a rocket, and changes course. The price of the maneuver is a loss of some mass, but it all comes down to the energy budget, not unseen substan
abstract: Warp drives from sci-fi can't easily change speed without exotic fuel. Scientists found a way using a "photon rocket" that pushes with light, so the drive steers by losing mass, like a rocket burning fuel. It turns out steering a warp bubble doesn't need negative energy—just a big enough fuel budget. Imagine a cosmic ship that sails by shining a headlight backward!

title: Moon Dust May Reveal Alien Technologies
oneliner: Lunar dust may hide microscopic messages from alien civilizations.
description: Calculations show that tiny artificial particles could cross interstellar space and survive in the lunar soil. Sunlight filters out the unsuitable ones, and on the Moon, they don't burn up in an atmosphere. Even a handful of regolith could tell us how often technological civilizations arose in the G
abstract: What if traces of alien technology lie in lunar dust? Research shows that tiny artificial particles from distant stars can wander through space and accumulate on the Moon. Like searching for needles in a haystack, scientists propose sifting through a cubic meter of lunar soil for such grains. Could the Moon hold keys to ancient galactic civilizations?

title: Quantum Drones Scan Ruins with Magnetic Vision
oneliner: Drones with quantum magnetometers can map voids in collapsed buildings to find survivors faster.
description: After earthquakes or explosions, every hour counts. New research proposes using drones with incredibly sensitive quantum magnetometers to peer beneath rubble. Scientists simulated the collapse of a concrete parking structure and showed that weak magnetic fields from steel rebar can reveal where void
abstract: Imagine a drone with magnetic vision, able to peer under rubble. Scientists have tested an ultra-sensitive magnetic mapping method to find voids where survivors might be—like an X-ray for rescue teams. The technology has already proven itself in collapse simulations. Could atomic sensitivity hold the key to saving lives?

title: The Chameleon Star: Where Did Theta Eridani's Brightness Go?
oneliner: Ancient astronomers saw Theta Eridani blazing, but today it’s barely visible. The answer lies in a cosmic dance between two stars.
description: Astronomers discovered that the Theta Eridani system contains three stars, with two inner ones locked in a tight dance. One star ran out of hydrogen fuel, swelled up, and wrapped its companion in a gaseous blanket. Friction inside this cloud triggered a bright flash that lasted for centuries. The fi
abstract: Ancient astronomers saw Theta Eridani as one of the brightest stars, but today it is nearly 10 times dimmer. It turns out it's a binary system where the stars are so close they almost merge — as if in a cosmic dance. This closeness temporarily heated the star, creating the illusion of super-brightness. What else are the stars hiding?

title: Time Loop from an Empty Universe
oneliner: Physicists built a model where closed time-like trajectories emerge from ordinary vacuum—without exotic matter.
description: Scientists have found an exact solution to Einstein's equations for empty space in which paths to the past arise spontaneously. It was once thought that time machines required exotic matter with negative energy, but the new work gets by with just a gravitational wave. This brings us closer to unders
abstract: Physicists have cracked an exact solution to Einstein’s equations for a vacuum, where closed time loops—a time machine—pop into existence from a totally normal starting point, without violating any known physics. Imagine walking in a straight line, only to end up back in your own past. Could this be a glitch-free gateway to time travel?

title: Radio Message from When the Universe Was Young
oneliner: An ancient radio signal reveals how galaxies grew when the cosmos was just 3 billion years old.
description: A brief radio flash came from a galaxy that existed soon after the Big Bang. The James Webb Space Telescope helped pinpoint its source—a small, clumpy cluster of stars bustling with star birth. This is the first flash traced to an object at such a distance, and it now allows us to study invisible ga
abstract: Fast Radio Bursts are mysterious millisecond signals from space. They are like echoes through which we can 'see' the invisible gas and magnetic fields of the Universe. Scientists found a new burst, FRB 20240304B, which reached us from just 3 billion years after the Big Bang, when galaxies were just forming. How many more secrets do these cosmic flashes hold?

title: Why Does Time Only Move Forward?
oneliner: A new explanation: it's all about the expansion and shape of the Universe.
description: A cup of coffee cools down but never heats up on its own—that's the arrow of time at work. Scientists have discovered: the direction from past to future depends not only on increasing disorder but also on how the cosmos expands.
abstract: The arrow of time—our feeling that events flow from past to future—might not be a local illusion, but a property of the entire Universe, depending on its geometry and topology. Just as the shape of a riverbed directs the water, the global structure of the cosmos sets the direction of time. So the key to understanding time’s flow lies in the fabric of the universe.

title: First Stars Found: Webb Peers into the Universe's Childhood
oneliner: The James Webb Space Telescope has for the first time captured light from stars of pure hydrogen and helium — probably the very first after the Big Bang.
description: The James Webb Space Telescope has for the first time captured light from stars consisting solely of hydrogen and helium — exactly what the very first stars after the Big Bang were probably made of. A cosmic magnifying glass helped: a galaxy cluster whose gravity amplified the faint shine of the dis
abstract: Using the James Webb Space Telescope, scientists have found a star from the first generation — like a fossil from the universe's youth. It's visible thanks to a gravitational lens that magnified the distant light. Its properties strikingly match what astronomers expected from the very first stars. Could we be peering into the dawn of time?

title: Quantum Switch Breaks the Usual Flow of Time
oneliner: Physicists have proven that cause and effect can swap places — and you don't have to trust the instruments to see it.
description: In everyday life, everything happens in order: cause, then effect. But quantum particles can exist in two temporal orders at once — as if time for them can flow both forward and backward simultaneously. Scientists tested this with a quantum switch and confirmed it by looking only at the final statis
abstract: Can we imagine a world where events don’t have a strict ‘cause and effect’ order? In quantum physics, this is possible: at the micro level, events can occur in superposition — as if simultaneously ‘before’ and ‘after’. Scientists have proven this for the first time without any assumptions about measurement devices, using entangled photons. The experiment violated an inequality, confirming that causality can be indefinite. So where is the boundary between reality and paradox?

title: Born Misaligned: Why Planets Don't Dance to Their Star's Tune
oneliner: Every third young sun-like star spins out of sync with its future planets from birth — like a top that forgot how to spin a hoop.
description: Astronomers peeked into planetary cradles and saw: in a third of cases, the disk of gas and dust is tilted relative to its star. That means worlds are born on crooked orbits, not acquiring chaos later. The discovery explains why even our system is slightly lopsided, and promises a whole menagerie of

title: The Mystery of the Gamma-Ray Burst: When the Speed of Light Is Not Constant
oneliner: Telescopes caught a photon from a gamma-ray burst that arrived late and wasn't absorbed — casting doubt on the constancy of the speed of light.
description: The brightest gamma-ray burst GRB 221009A produced a photon with the energy of a flying mosquito. It arrived an hour and a half late and didn't scatter on background light, although physics laws demand otherwise. Perhaps the speed of light changes with energy.
abstract: The Carpet-3 facility caught an ultra-high-energy photon from a gamma-ray burst that arrived very late and didn't weaken, though it should have. It's like a sunbeam piercing thick fog without losing brightness. To explain the puzzle, scientists allowed that the properties of light might change slightly at extreme speeds. Could this particle open a door to new physics?

title: The Quantum Mpemba Mystery: When Greater Chaos Speeds Up Order
oneliner: Physicists have controlled the quantum Mpemba effect on a chip for the first time: a more disordered system orders itself faster.
description: Hot water freezes faster than cold—that's the Mpemba effect. In the quantum world, its counterpart is a system with a highly broken symmetry restoring order faster. Scientists controlled this on a superconducting chip, turning an accelerated 'tightening' of disorder on and off. This could help quant
abstract: Just as hot water sometimes freezes faster than cold, the Mpemba effect operates in the quantum world: a system with a stronger symmetry breaking restores it faster. Scientists not only observed this on a superconducting processor but also learned to control the effect, turning it on and off. Can chaos speed up order?

title: Life Without Water: Warm Salty Worlds
oneliner: On planets where water has long since turned to vapour, life might rely on molten salt—this discovery pushes the boundaries of the search for habitable worlds.
description: The search for life has typically focused on planets with liquid water. But hot exoplanets lack it. It turns out that ionic liquids—molten salts that don't evaporate in hellish heat—could substitute for water. Scientists created them in the lab from volcanic acid and organic matter. So, there may be
abstract: Some planets are too hot for liquid water, but life might thrive in puddles of molten salt — ionic liquids. They barely evaporate, even on dry planets with thin atmospheres. Experiments show that such 'salt puddles' could form from naturally occurring substances. Could this reshape our view of worlds fit for life?

title: Tiny Giants: Black Holes Grow from Primordial Seeds
oneliner: Invisible 'seeds' from the Big Bang era could have grown into giants caught by gravitational wave observatories.
description: The merger of two unusually heavy black holes — GW231123 — suggests they might have originated from primordial seeds. Over billions of years, they sucked in matter, gaining mass and spinning up. Future observations will test this hypothesis and perhaps reveal the nature of dark matter.
abstract: Scientists have found the heaviest pair of black holes ever observed: they are too massive and spin too fast to have formed from ordinary stars. Perhaps these black holes were born in the early Universe and grew like a snowball, pulling in matter — this explains both their mass and their spin speed. Could this be evidence for the existence of primordial black holes?

title: Mechanical Schrödinger's Cat
oneliner: Physicists found a simple way to make a nanostring vibrate forward and backward at the same time — just one qubit and a clever signal.
description: Instead of complex setups — just one qubit (an artificial atom) and a resonator. A signal at double the frequency swings the string in two opposite directions at once, like the legendary cat, only mechanical. This simplifies experiments and paves the way for ultra-precise sensors and error-proof qua
abstract: Scientists have figured out a way to create a Schrödinger's cat quantum state for a tiny mechanical part, using just one control qubit. Just as pushing a swing in sync boosts its amplitude, here driving at double the frequency births pairs of vibrations that add up into a macroscopic superposition. This opens the door to compact quantum sensors and information protection. Could an ordinary resonator live in two realities at once?

title: Speed Outsmarts the Laws of Heat Engines
oneliner: Moving near light speed lets quantum sensors squeeze more thermal energy, breaking old efficiency limits.
description: A heat engine made of quantum particles, flying almost as fast as light, perceives temperature in a whole new way. Einstein’s effects make the hot even hotter and the cold even colder, boosting useful work and bypassing the classical barrier. Speed becomes a thermodynamic trump card.
abstract: Imagine two tiny sensors moving through hot and cold environments. Because of their motion, each feels its own temperature, just like a runner feels a stronger headwind. This 'temperature deception' lets a miniature quantum engine operate with greater output and even bypass usual physical limits. What if motion itself is the key to new energy sources?

title: Diamond DNA Chip: A Revolution in Rapid Diagnostics
oneliner: This diamond chip needs no chemistry: it senses 49 disease markers by their magnetic whispers.
description: A diamond plate with 49 DNA sensors catches disease marker molecules. When the target is caught, the magnetic noise quiets, and the sensor gives a signal—fast, with no reagents or bulky equipment.
abstract: Scientists have created a diamond chip that simultaneously recognizes dozens of biological molecules—much like locks that only open with their own keys. When a target molecule finds its 'lock,' the chip sends a quantum signal. This technology promises to make medical tests faster, cheaper, and more accurate. Soon, complex tests might be done right at the patient's bedside.

title: Motion as Fuel: Quantum Engines at Near-Light Speeds
oneliner: Near-light-speed motion of reservoirs allows a quantum heat engine to operate with efficiency above the theoretical maximum.
description: Researchers have shown that if the heat and cold sources in a quantum maser (microwave amplifier) race along at nearly the speed of light, this engine circumvents the classical efficiency limit. Moreover, it can operate even without a temperature difference—the motion itself serves as fuel.
abstract: Imagine a quantum engine that runs not on temperature differences but on fast motion. When its parts move at enormous speeds, the radiation spectrum shifts — just like a siren’s sound changes as a car approaches. This lets you get more energy than usual. So, can you extract work even from thermal equilibrium, simply by moving?

title: Tiny chip tames quantum entanglement
oneliner: Scientists have built a source of entangled light particles into a fingernail-sized chip powered by a regular battery.
description: The device generates pairs of entangled photons at a record rate—45 billion per second—consuming just milliwatts of power. The purity of the quantum connection exceeds 96%. Its tiny size and efficiency bring quantum internet, secure satellite links, and ultra-precise sensors closer to reality.
abstract: Scientists have placed a source of quantum twins—entangled photons—onto a tiny chip that runs on a battery. It pumps out pairs at the speed needed for ultra-secure communication. Perhaps these very chips will soon make our video calls completely private.

title: Entangled Photons vs. Murky Media
oneliner: Scientists used quantum entanglement to transmit images through scattering media that completely obscure ordinary light.
description: A new method uses pairs of entangled photons to transmit sharp images through murky media—places where ordinary light is helpless. The medium remains opaque to everyone except the quantum duo, opening the door to ultra-secure communication and improved medical and astronomical imaging.
abstract: Light in a murky medium scatters, blurring images—like looking through frosted glass. Scientists found a way to send a clear picture through such a medium using entangled photon pairs (light particles that are linked). Ordinary light stays scattered, but the quantum signal passes cleanly—like a secret code readable only with a special “key.” What else could we hide in entangled light?

title: Time Crystals Boost Batteries and Sensors
oneliner: Chains of magnets oscillating in their own rhythm can store energy and measure time with precision beyond the quantum limit.
description: A chain of tiny magnets, where coupling strength drops sharply with distance, under rhythmic kicks transitions into a discrete time crystal state. Energy accumulates avalanche-like, and sensitivity to rhythm disruptions surpasses all known limits.
abstract: Imagine a battery that gets more efficient as it gets bigger, and at the same time works as a supersensitive sensor. Scientists have found a quantum system with a special type of interaction that behaves exactly like that: it stores energy with reserves and catches time shifts more accurately than any known limits. Isn't this bringing us closer to the technologies of the future?

title: Search for alien radio signals along the planetary plane
oneliner: Earthly transmissions have shown: to catch an alien signal, you need to look where planets line up.
description: Analysis of 20 years of NASA antenna operations shows that the most powerful terrestrial radio signals are emitted in the plane of the Solar System. If extraterrestrial civilizations follow the same logic, their transmissions should be sought right there, especially when a planet passes in front of
abstract: Scientists studied where and when Earth's antennas beam signals into space. It turns out our signals, like a flashlight beam, are most often directed along the solar system's plane and at times when the planets line up. So if we want to hear from an alien civilization, we should look exactly there and at those same moments. So when's the best time to wave to our invisible neighbors?

title: Magnetic Brakes for Cosmic Spinning Tops
oneliner: How magnetic fields, like brakes, control the mass and spin of black holes, explaining the strange findings of gravitational wave detectors.
description: Scientists simulated the birth of black holes from massive stars and saw that magnetic fields act as a cosmic brake. A strong field slows the hole's spin, a weak one spins it up to near-light speed. This explained why detectors occasionally see pairs of heavyweight black holes, like the record-break
abstract: Scientists have figured out that stellar magnetic fields play a key role in the birth of record-breaking black holes. If the field is strong, it acts like a brake, slowing the hole's spin and keeping its mass in the "forbidden" range. With a weak field, the black hole spins up to nearly the speed of light. This could explain puzzling mergers like GW231123. Are they accompanied by ultra-powerful gamma-ray bursts?

title: Precise tuning of quantum gates without compromising readout
oneliner: Physicists achieved record precision in all key operations of a quantum processor simultaneously.
description: A new tuning technique made it possible to combine ultra-precise single-qubit and two-qubit operations with high-quality readout. Previously, one harmed the other, but now a balance has been found. This is an important step toward computers that can correct their own errors.
abstract: Quantum computers are like an orchestra: every note must be pure. Engineers tuned the connections between qubits so that all operations — solos, duets, and reading the notes — are performed with over 99.9% accuracy. Like a conductor, they found the perfect balance without sacrificing quality. Now the orchestra is ready to play the most complex symphonies without a false note — can you imagine this is the path to error-free quantum computing?

title: Interstellar Visitors: Searching for Alien Technology
oneliner: Scientists are preparing to greet interstellar guests—and search them for signs of alien technology.
description: The Vera Rubin Telescope will discover thousands of interstellar visitors. To tell a natural object from a relic of another civilization, scientists have developed a protocol: they’ll study the light 'fingerprints' of these guests.
abstract: Scientists have already found three interstellar objects that have wandered into the Solar System. We can check them for traces of alien technology—like messages in bottles tossed into the cosmic ocean. A new powerful telescope will soon find thousands of such visitors, and we need to figure out beforehand how to look for artificial ones. What if one of them turns out to be a creation of another intelligence?

title: How Twisted Light Reveals a Black Hole's Charge
oneliner: A new method uses the rotation of light beams to measure the electric charge of the supermassive black hole M87*.
description: Light bending around a black hole gets twisted — by the spiraling of the beams you can measure its electric charge. Astrophysicists applied this approach to images of the M87 galaxy and obtained an upper limit for the first time: the hole is almost neutral. The method is universal and suitable for h
abstract: Astrophysicists have figured out how to measure the electric charge of the supermassive black hole M87* by watching how its light twists. Just as the shape of a whirlpool reveals the strength of the current, the spinning light carries clues about the black hole's properties. The analysis showed the charge doesn't exceed 39% of its mass. Could twisted light unlock other mysteries of gravity?
